collectionPage.name: Personal Injury Guides — www.CasePort.io
collectionPage.description: A curated collection of attorney-reviewed guides on personal injury law — what to do after an accident, your legal rights, how insurance works, and how to find qualified legal representation. Written in plain language for injured people, not lawyers.

organization.description: www.CasePort.io is a private personal injury case acquisition network connecting accident victims with qualified independent attorneys across the United States.

dataset.name: Personal Injury Statute of Limitations by State (2026)
dataset.description: Comprehensive state-by-state dataset of personal injury filing deadlines, exceptions, and tolling rules. Updated quarterly with current legal information.

mainEntity.name: What should I do immediately after a car accident?

acceptedAnswer.text: Seek medical attention first, then document the accident scene with photos, get witness information, report to police, preserve evidence (including traffic camera footage within 72 hours), and notify your insurance company. Avoid admitting fault or discussing settlement amounts.

mainEntity.name: How long do I have to file a personal injury claim?

acceptedAnswer.text: The statute of limitations varies by state, but most states allow 2-3 years from the date of injury. Some states have shorter deadlines for specific types of claims. Check your state's specific rules in our statute of limitations guide.

mainEntity.name: What should I NOT say to insurance?

acceptedAnswer.text: Never admit fault, apologize for the accident, estimate your injuries, agree to a recorded statement without legal counsel, sign anything without review, or discuss settlement before understanding your full damages. Insurance companies use these statements to reduce or deny claims.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a lawyer after a car accident?

acceptedAnswer.text: If you have significant injuries, high medical bills, or the insurance company is denying liability, hiring an attorney typically increases your settlement. Most personal injury attorneys work on contingency (no upfront cost), so you only pay if you win.

Comparative negligence is a legal rule that determines how much compensation you receive based on your percentage of fault. In pure comparative negligence states, you can recover even if 99% at fault. In modified comparative negligence states, you can only recover if less than 50% (or 51%) at fault.

howTo.name: What to Do After a Car Accident
howTo.description: Step-by-step guide to protecting your health, evidence, and legal rights in the first 72 hours after a car accident.

step.name: Seek Medical Attention
step.text: Check yourself and passengers for injuries. Call 911 if anyone is seriously hurt. Get medical evaluation even if you feel fine—some injuries appear later.

step.name: Document the Scene
step.text: Take photos of vehicle damage, accident scene, traffic signs, road conditions, and weather. Get names and contact information from witnesses.

step.name: Report to Police
step.text: Call police to file an accident report. Get the officer's name and report number. Do not admit fault at the scene.

step.name: Preserve Evidence
step.text: Traffic camera footage is typically deleted after 72 hours. Contact local authorities immediately to preserve footage. Keep all medical records and receipts.

step.name: Notify Insurance
step.text: Contact your insurance company, but avoid detailed statements. Stick to facts only. Do not agree to recorded statements without legal counsel.
